titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 23 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, lady Lillian Spender (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender states that he is getting 'a little tired' of sending Christmas wishes, mentions the visit a comrade, Richardson, and suggests that one possible plan for his (Spender's) upcoming leave could be for himself and Lillian to go to a house in Wiltshire owned by his brother Rex. Spender refers to a speech by David Lloyd George (1863-1945), the then Minister for Munitions, in which he suggested that the war could continue for another year, or more. Spender urges Lillian not to tire herself out writing letters to him, and gives news of the progress of the fighting.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
23 Dec 15 My pr I believe this is really my Xmas letter but I am getting
a little tired of sending Xmas greetings even to a pr when I know
she now takes them â & a lot of other things too â quite for granted.
However I'll wish her one more wish which I know, & hope it
may come true. Richardson is off & I am sorry that as he will only be
one day in town he will not be able to look you up, tho' he took
your tel No in case there may be a chance later. I think
his wife who lives at 18 Park Avenue Bidford & who often comes
up to town (!) would probably be glad to see you, but probably
not till after he returns. I wonder if Rex's old place in the New Forest would
do for us. I'm not quite sure of the address but think it
was Mrs Olding. Olding's farm Redlynch near Downton
Wiltshire. It is a delightful part of the country & a comfortable
farm. It might be worth writing (?) & if you do so don't
forget to enquire the best means of getting there. I'm afraid I am
giving a tired little pr a great deal to do & to decide, but as
she knows the will not mind what she does with him, as
long as she ......, she need not worry about troubling
unduly in making up her mind.
I see that Lloyd George's speech
makes it quite plain that he
does not expect the war to finish in less that 1 year & that
Redmond is beginning to show his teeth more in the open. I retract
anything I have said about his possibly being an honest patriot. Squiffy will try to put things off again on hearing his master's voice
but I have some faint hope that this may prove too much for L G
& that the former may have to go but I'm afraid this is too
good to be true & there is always the his Â£9000 or Â£10000 to think
of. If I were a millionaire I think I would pension him off
for the sake of the country tho' few men ( or women ) deserve it
less. I don't like your being so tired now writing me letters, I
quite understand about the times. All I mean to say is that
if you have anything urgent for the post it is best to post it
a good deal earlier in the afternoon is possible but I don't want
you to worry about the letters for me any more than you
want me to do for you. The place I was at in the trenches was heavily shelled just
after I left ( ? my motor) but no one was hurt & the village itself
was past hurting. News of a big French victory ( at any rate
it sounds like a bit victory with 1200 prisoners ) has just come in.
It is very necessary that the French should get encouragement just now My dear love to the family & best Xmas wishes!! Your bw WB Spender
